speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Rachel Huber. It's the 28th of May, 1995. Rachel, you were talking about the and the plan to get to Tel Aviv and get as many people off as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What actually happened on that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: What actually happened was that the English-- the English intelligence got to know about the plan. And the-- one English cruiser or whatever, I don't even know what-- if it was a cruiser, but it was one English ship followed us all along, because they knew of our plans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they follow you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very far from Saint-Chamas they appeared. As we came out from France, they were there. So we knew that our chances are lessened. But we sort of though that we will be taken Cyprus, as all the others were. As we came-- as we came down to where today is Gaza.

speaker: interviewer
text: How How long did that take, that trip across the Mediterranean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight days. Eight days. Eight days we were on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brutal. Brutal I would say. There was enough water and there was enough food for whoever could eat. It was very, very hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was it? What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was June, was end-- end of May to June. We had a great number of pregnant women. We had a great number of children there. And the discomfort was very, very great, very great. The sea-- the ship on itself, I can't say it was not seaworthy, but it was very near to not being seaworthy. We were still in-- in the international waters when on an evening-- on the eighth evening, the English we heard with very big loud speakers talking to us that they know that we are on the way to Palestine and they know that we are illegal immigrants, that we should follow them wherever they would take us-- they will take us in-- that the ship should follow them. The captain answered that too. We are international waters and so on. But little did they-- when they came to the conclusion that we are not heeding their advice or command or however you'd want to take it, they rammed the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rammed the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are who? The British?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British. They rammed the ship, tearing out a hole into the body of the ship. The water went in and people were standing until here in water, the people that were on the lowest deck. The-- before-- I forgot something to tell. Before that evening, the captain knew most probably from further-- from before experience. We took-- [SIGHS] [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what they-- I'm sorry. I forgot a word in English. That we put that around so the English couldn't get a hold on. Sometimes you have-- on borders you have-- from-- wire that has--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire. Barbed wire. We took barbed wire, we put around that they couldn't jump onto the ship. But it didn't take them long. We had the children organized on the side of the ship, with tins, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tins of meat or whatever it was, just conserves, when they will attack us, to throw that in. Anyhow, there was a battle that was maybe 1 and 1/2 to 2 hours, until the English just threw in tear gas and we didn't see anything before our eyes. And they had taken from tires-- they had the inside of a tire. They had put it here on their arms, and with a baton so that nobody could hit them. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The English put down like a bit of a tire, like part of a tire, put inside-- it was probably they had wood there to hold it-- so that nobody could-- you throw a tine and it just-- just doesn't harm you. And they had batons. And they came up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people injured?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 1 and 1/2 hours somehow of battle. And they took the ship up to Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, were people injured in this battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were-- I think there were two dead. I think a child was-- died. And there were several of the children were wounded. Afterwards when they were already masters of the ship, they tried to go around and to give something to drink to those who were injured. But nobody wanted to look at them. But a interesting thing was the captain, which was a young man, maybe 23 or 24, happened to come in into the hospital place where my father was. And he took a shirt that Shmuelik wore, because he had to change. And he should be one of the-- one of the people, one of the mass, so they wouldn't catch him. Well, you couldn't believe that he, that young man, is a captain of a ship. And apparently afterwards, he was really-- gave him a medal in Israel or whatever. He had afterwards a very big part in building up the Navy, the Israeli Navy. And when we arrived in Haifa, when we saw Haifa from the far, I will never forget the Hatikvah that was sang then. It was the most tearful, the most screaming Hatikvah that can be. All those people after all-- everyone with his bundle of suffering that he went through. And here you see Palestine and-- and the English are at your back. But we still hoped that they will take us to Cyprus. When we have arrived at the shore, we saw that in Haifa, there were no people from the far as one could see. One could only see on the roof, because in Haifa, the roofs are flat, so people could stand. And you saw people waving from very, very far, what you-- whatever you-- they eye can discern. And then, an hour or two after we were in Haifa-- arrived in Haifa, they gave us pieces of leaflets, which I will like to show you, in which there were instructions, that everything that we have, that means the knapsacks that we have, should be left behind, and we should disembark. And they put to our disposal three English ships, and they will take us further. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did not want to disembark. We were helped by the English batons to disembark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were pushed [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --pushed off.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Certain children they took and carried them down. When we came down, we were shown directly into-- straight behind the was a ship standing, an English ship. And we were shown up to go on that ship. The name of the ship was called When we came in, what we thought-- what we saw was again that was a fully prepared ship for carrying [INAUDIBLE]. Everything was taken out from inside, and again bunks putting up. And we were shown there, we get into a bunk. The people of the-- we were, as I mentioned before, 4,500 on the And they divided it in the three ships, 1,400 in each and every ship. A few hours later, the ships started moving. To much to our disappointment, we couldn't explain it, we saw us moving away from the sun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that mean that we're we're going westward. We were supposed to go a bit westward for Cypress, because Cypress is westward from-- from Palestine. But we though that in two, three hours we would be in-- in Cyprus. We were steaming, going and going and going and going. And it took a day. And another day we made already a hunger strike. The captain came on the loudspeaker or whatever he did and told us, it's not going to help you anything if you're on hunger strike.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality was the captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: English. English. Was the English army. He said-- that was an army ship. He said to us, I don't know where I go by instructions. I do not know where I am instructed to go. I will find out, then I'll let you know. In the meantime, if you are making a hunger strike, you will be hungry, not I. And that was the message. We had one day hunger strike. The next day, we got-- got some biscuits with the worms were crawling on that. And there was no yielding from the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I honestly believe that he did not know, because in the meantime, there was a colossal pressure on the English government. It started to mount-- mount a pressure on the English government, where would they take us, because the Jewish agency knew that we were supposed to be in Cypress. If we didn't arrive in Cypress, the red light already went on, where do they take us?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, the English had Aneruin Bevan was-- was the foreign minister, a person that excelled himself and his hatred to anything that was Jewish and anything that had to do with Palestine. He was extremely pro-Arab. And he just thought that he will crush us as he crushed others. And he decided to take us back to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: After again get eight days that we were on the we arrived in Port-de-Bouc was the name of the place where we arrived. We arrived om Port-de-Bouc. And there, the captain says, you are ready for the [INAUDIBLE] embarkation. That's how you say, I suppose. Certainly that we did not want to disembark in France. Where are we going to go? Who wants to go? We had a few-- on the ship, we had a few young men that were French-speaking. They came from Algiers or Morocco, I don't know, from North Africa anyhow. And they stepped-- stepped forward. Yes. I-- I think two or three days after arriving in France, we had visitors, a group, a Parliamentarian groups from Paris came with some I do not know who they were from the government, came down the steps, looked around on that sight. It wasn't a very pleasant sight. We were all half-- half-clad, because the heat was enormous and we didn't have much breathing space. Everybody took off whatever. The-- the men were just in underwear, because they just couldn't bear it. They looked at the sight. And they read to us in French and then somebody translated in English that they offered us French citizenship as a-- as a sign of their goodwill or so on, because they know that we are refugees and so on. We should disembark. And then one of us, that one particular young Frenchman, stepped forward and explained to them that we are refugees, we are Jewish, and we thank them for their hospitality that they want to offer us. But our way really is to go to our homeland and not in another land again, and nor do we want to be visitors again somewhere else. They said that they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] report that to their government. And that was-- and that was it. There was a colossal pressure to disembark, a moral pressure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Really, we had one Hungarian family that disembarked, much to the phooey and to the-- the whistles of everybody inside. But they decided that they can't bear it anymore and they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who organized the-- the Jewish refugees on that boat? I mean, when you say we decided, we did this, who actually made the decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, there was a committee. There was-- there always seemed all through the-- all through the way, all through the whole saga of wandering, we always had a committee that people can choose for. And somehow, a leader evolved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody that has-- I don't know his name. I wouldn't know. But he had contact with somebody outside-- outside the ship, that-- that he would relay and would get. Most probably, we assumed-- I do not know if that's true, that while in Haifa, that somebody smuggled himself in to organize the resistance. But that is something that was rumored and which I do not know if it's true or not. We never knew which one it is. But the committee knew who-- who he is. The very small committee knew how to-- the organization was perfect, because if everybody would know who it is, then the English will find out somehow under pressure, under-- under bribe or I do not know what. One had to be sure that the English do not find out. While we were on the ship-- we were six weeks anchored in Port-de-Bouc.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon as we arrived in Port-de-Bouc, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish help, who was it? Was it the Joint? Was it an organization of French Jews? We never knew exactly which part. It was not really important. The important was that we were not alone. The important was that the Jewish world listens, knows about us, are behind us, and help us. That was all that was important for us. We got bread was delivered, long fresh French breads. Inside we had-- were baked in those notes. We are with you. There were-- we always knew what's going on in the outside world, because in bread or in food or in somewhere, there were always notes. And whatever-- if whoever got the note, we always gave it immediately--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were-- did you have these messages smuggled in constantly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They were a colossal moral booster-- colossal moral booster. At that time, the head of the Haganah was Moshe Sneh. One day, there was a rumor. There is a boat outside and it's Moshe Sneh. And whoever had the luck to be on fresh air at that time, saw him in the boat around that, calling out, we are with you. Don't give in. It will come to an end. The pressure must go on and so on. There were rumors that Golda Meir was in one of the boats. There were boats coming around just giving signs or-- or-- or shouting out something. That was enough for us to be encouraged. That went on for six weeks. It was a very hard time to keep up the-- the morale of the elderly people, of people that were really physically suffering. A young person can take--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Very, very difficult. You wouldn't believe it. But I had with me a hot water bottle that I had taken with in the rucksack. Instead others took maybe a dress or whatever. I took a hard water bottle, because my father's angina was quite severe. And by applying a hot water bottle here, it used to dissolve the cramp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Most probably today maybe a cardiologist would say, goodness, gracious? What are you doing? But it helped him. Him-- he reacted on that. So I had a hot water bottle. And I used to come to the barbed wire and ask the-- our guard, the English soldiers, to fill it with hot water. And they used to do it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used [INAUDIBLE]-- I knew a few words. I learned French in school. I learned Latin in school. I learned-- I knew German. I knew Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when one knows all those languages, English comes a bit easier. So I used to ask them they should give me hot water for my father. And they used to give me, so I used to apply those hot water bottles.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a hard time, I will confess. But when you have got a purpose and you've got-- you are young and you want to succeed and you want to come to your aim, you do it. And I had to keep up my parents. After eight days-- after six weeks, sorry. I'm sorry. After six weeks, suddenly they started to depart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went westward again. We went down to the Spanish coast, through Gibraltar. In Gibraltar we stopped, because they had to bring water and supply or whatever. And in Gibraltar, you wouldn't believe it-- I did not know it. Nobody knew that there was a Jewish community in Gibraltar. And the Jewish community from Gibraltar sent us up beautiful food, pears I can remember, and bread. And again, everywhere there was a message, keep firm. Keep tough. Just-- just keep on. And-- and that was again a colossal encouragement. Gibraltar seemed from-- from the-- behind the barbed wire, it seemed a beautiful, beautiful place, Gibraltar, to be. But behind the barbed wire is not a real place to-- to look at something. And then we went up the Portuguese cost, saw the La Manche canal, up the [INAUDIBLE]. That was already autumn. It was already cold. All we had on ourselves were torn clothes from the battle on the from all that we were down. The ship was going up and down, because it was not that the newest stabilized one. After the La Manche canal, the East-- the [INAUDIBLE] is quite moving, quite--

speaker: interviewer
text: Choppy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Choppy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And it was not a pleasure at all. We arrived in Bremen after I don't know how many days, Six, seven days. I don't know how many days. I can't remember that anymore. We were quite miserable, cold and shivering. And the battle came for disembarkation. Again, the same-- the same awful sights. They-- pulling us down, pushing us down. I got a few batons on my-- on my leg. Move, move, move. The whole thing again. We were put in very big trucks and we were taken [SIGHS] to a place called Poppendorf. That was the name of the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was in-- in a province in Germany called Friesland. That is northwest part of Germany. Very cold already there. Again, in something that must have been before some form of a camp, because they had a watchtower there and they had barbed wire in-- around it. And the English were standing on the watchtower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded you all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English. The English now. Now, we arrived I don't know what day it was. After 1 or 1 and 1/2 day, in came enormous trucks with green blankets, army blankets, beautiful thick army blankets that the Joint sent us to cover, because they knew that we were with torn clothes-- that they sent us that we should have being able to cover. Thousands and thousands of blankets they sent in, just we should have with what to cover us up, because there was no one covering in the-- in those camps. [SIGHS] After a few days, the English changed their policy. They disappeared completely. The army disappeared. The watchtower, everything disappeared. They left us alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was behind it? Very clear. We should disobey, everybody will go then different place, and then they will have solved-- solved the problem. Certainly that we didn't let it that way. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We immediately-- we immediately decided-- first the committee talked to the people and explained that if what we went through would go all for nothing if we will dissipate, everybody going to another place in Germany. Going back even to Foehrenwald, what would we have achieved in all that? And then we would give to the English people-- the English government just a trump card.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, we organized ourselves, explaining to the people not to go on. And then what we decided was that we have to give out [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] identity cards. But identity cards sound somehow not the right thing, a card that acknowledges that you have been on so that wherever people-- whatever will come, we did not know, that people should be able to show to the Jewish agency that we were on So some people had cameras, so immediately started to make photos of the people so every-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every identity card should have the-- the photo, not the name, because I-- we didn't want to divulge names if it will come in English hands by some way. So everybody had a number, a photo, and a-- and a to whom it may concern that that person was on I still have got my card and I will show it to you later. Did you stay at Poppendorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In Poppendorf we stayed for a short while.

speaker: interviewer
text: All together.

speaker: interviewee
text: All together. And then it started a relocating us. That now was already organized from the Jewish agency. We were relocated to [INAUDIBLE] in a particular place. And then, part of that young people were taken-- were taken away to another place, because they hoped that they will have-- at that time, there was [SIGHS] a talk that the English government will give 100,00 certificates. And that was what we call in Hebrew [HEBREW]. That was a negotiation. And so they took the very young ones out. That was Jewish agency already organizing, re-organizing the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go with your-- with your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- we-- Shmuelik and I, my parents, we went to Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen was already at that time a displaced person camp, not the concentration camp from before. And it was a very big displaced persons camp that had a very good hospital on there. And my father was physically so rundown from-- from all that that we went through that he had to have medical attention. So what they-- what the-- the Jewish agency organized that we should be accepted in-- in Bergen-Belsen camp. We got a room there, and my father was immediately accepted to hospital. And my father was six months in Bergen-Belsen hospital. He went-- all sorts of injection he was given. All sorts of-- until they habilitated him to his former self, so to speak. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my father, in the meantime-- in the meantime, it was 1948 and it was the-- the Declaration of Independence in Israel. We were in Bergen-Belsen when we heard the Declaration of Independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you listen to the UN count?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did we? Did we? I counted with them. We counted with them together. We counted them. The jubilation-- jubilation at camp is not like the jubilation anywhere else, because all those people are crying and-- and laughing together. But you cry what you went through and you laugh for what it is now. And then it started already. And then they were all-- the people from had first acceptance or first-- they were in queue the first to go to Israel, because this was not anything illegal anymore. And unfortunately I must say-- unfortunately, a lot of young people that were on coming to Israel, because they were the first to come to Israel, were sent to Latrun. Latrun was a colossal battle with the-- the English and the Arabs. The English [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] left and [INAUDIBLE]. And a lot of people were killed in Latrun.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Israel in May 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.
